demands.rationale: NYC restaurants pay premium for ultra-local microgreens; AeroFarms and Bowery already at capacity.

sources.snippet: Manhattan restaurants pay $60-80/lb wholesale for sunflower and pea microgreens.

demands.rationale: Italian-American restaurant density (Manhattan, Brooklyn) plus retail demand from Whole Foods and FreshDirect.

sources.snippet: Wholesale basil prices in NYC up 35% in 2025 due to import disruptions and local demand growth.

demands.rationale: California romaine recalls in 2024-2025 drove buyers to seek local hydroponic alternatives. Whole Foods, Wegmans expanding NY-grown lines.

sources.snippet: Repeated E. coli recalls have pushed retailers to allocate 15-20% of leafy-green shelf space to local hydroponic.

demands.rationale: California water restrictions push wholesale prices up; year-round local production at premium margins.

sources.snippet: California strawberry production down 8% YoY due to water allocation cuts.

demands.rationale: Strong demand from Chinatown, Flushing, and Manhattan high-end Asian fine dining. Currently shipped from California or Mexico.

sources.snippet: Demand for Asian leafy greens in NYC restaurant supply has grown 14% annually since 2022.

demands.rationale: Year-round retail and food-service demand. Local hydroponic varieties capture premium price segment.

sources.snippet: NYC metro consumes ~5.5M kg/month cherry tomatoes; 60% imported from Mexico in winter.

demands.rationale: Eleven Madison Park, Le Bernardin, Atomix all source from local urban growers. Boutique and high-margin.

sources.snippet: Top NYC restaurants list ~3-4 edible-flower varieties per dish; supply is the bottleneck.

demands.rationale: Restaurant staple plus retail growth. Food-service distributors (Baldor, Driscoll's) report consistent volume demand.

sources.snippet: Arugula maintains top-3 position in NYC food-service leafy-green orders.
